title: Vietnam's 90-Day E-Visa: A Guide for Long-Term Travelers

interlinking_suggestions.title: Ho Chi Minh City, Vietnam: The Ultimate Digital Nomad Guide (2025)

author.name: Isabelle Rossi
author.bio: Having lived and worked in over 30 countries, Isabelle is a leading voice on the digital nomad movement. She covers everything from visa strategies and travel hacking to maintaining well-being on the road.

key_takeaways: Before booking a private rental, you must confirm the host is able and willing to complete the legally mandatory temporary residence registration with local police for you.
Meticulously track your cumulative days in Vietnam across all visits within a 12-month period to avoid crossing the 183-day threshold that triggers tax residency on your worldwide income.
Create an audit-proof record for tax purposes by photographing your passport's official entry and exit stamps, as they are irrefutable proof of your physical presence.
Always use the official government e-visa portal and cross-reference your flight booking with the approved list of entry ports to prevent a catastrophic denial of entry.
Choose the multiple-entry visa as a low-cost insurance policy for operational agility, allowing you to exit and re-enter for regional business without reapplying.
summary: Professionals using Vietnam's 90-day e-visa often face avoidable risks, including entry denial and unforeseen tax liabilities, by treating the process with a tourist's casualness. The core advice is to manage the entire stay as a strategic operation, meticulously documenting each phase from application to exit and ensuring compliance with local rules like temporary residence registration. This disciplined approach transforms reactive worry into proactive control, resulting in a seamless, productive stay and an audit-proof record for future financial and tax verification.
ai_overview_answer: Professionally managing Vietnam's 90-day e-visa requires a structured approach focused on compliant entry, in-country risk management, and a documented exit. Begin by applying only through the official government portal (`evisa.gov.vn`), selecting a multiple-entry visa for flexibility, and verifying your port of entry is on the approved list before booking flights. Once in Vietnam, your most critical task is ensuring your accommodation provider completes the mandatory temporary residence registration with local police and provides you with proof. You must also meticulously track your cumulative days in the country, as reaching 183 days within a tax year can make you a tax resident liable for tax on worldwide income. For a clean exit, create an audit-proof digital file containing your e-visa, inbound and outbound flight itineraries, and clear photos of your passport's entry and exit stamps. These official stamps are irrefutable proof of your physical presence, which is essential for defending your international status during future tax audits.

step.name: Phase 1: Secure a Flawless & Compliant Entry
step.text: Your operational success begins not when you land, but with the meticulous execution of your visa application. This initial phase is about establishing a clean, verifiable record from the very first step, mitigating risk before you ever board the plane.

itemListElement.text: Execute the Application with Precision: Use only the official government e-visa portal (`https://evisa.gov.vn` or `https://thithucdientu.gov.vn`) to avoid data vulnerabilities and extra fees. Ensure all details match your passport exactly.

Choose Your Entry Type Strategically: Opt for the multiple-entry visa (50 USD) as a low-cost insurance policy for operational agility, allowing you to leave and re-enter Vietnam without a new application.
Establish Your Digital 'Go-Bag': Immediately save your e-visa PDF, payment receipt, and confirmation email to a dedicated, cloud-synced folder for easy access and tax verification.
Verify Your Port of Entry Before Booking: Cross-reference your intended airport or land border with the official government list of 42 designated entry points to avoid being denied entry.

step.name: Phase 2: Manage Your In-Country Compliance Risks
step.text: With your flawless entry secured, the compliance landscape shifts from travel documents to your physical presence. This phase requires active management of two critical, often-overlooked risks: temporary residence registration and the activation of your global tax clock.

itemListElement.text: Master Temporary Residence Registration: Ensure your accommodation provider (hotel or landlord) registers your address with local police. Verify this has been done and get a photo of the form for your records.

Track Your Global Tax Residency Clock: Be aware of Vietnam's 183-day rule for tax residency. Meticulously track your cumulative days in the country across all visits within a tax year to avoid unintentionally becoming a tax resident.

step.name: Phase 3: Execute a Documented Exit for Future Audits
step.text: Your departure from Vietnam is your last chance to gather the official evidence needed to defend your international status against future scrutiny. This isn't just good record-keeping; it's about building an audit-proof case.

itemListElement.text: Build Your Audit-Proof Exit File: Consolidate five key documents in your digital folder: the e-visa PDF, inbound and outbound flight itineraries, and clear photos of your passport's entry and exit stamps.

Value Your Passport Stamps: An official government entry and exit stamp is irrefutable proof of your physical presence. Ensure the stamps are present and legible before leaving the immigration counter.

Reconcile Your Records Immediately: Within 48 hours of departure, update your master travel spreadsheet with the exact entry and exit dates from your passport stamps to ensure data integrity for tax filings.

mainEntity.name: How do I register my temporary residence in Vietnam as a foreigner?

acceptedAnswer.text: The legal obligation falls on your accommodation provider (hotel or landlord). For hotels, it's a standard part of check-in. For private rentals (e.g., Airbnb), you must confirm with the host *before booking* that they will handle the registration. Your responsibility is to verify it has been done and request a photo of the completed form for your records.

mainEntity.name: Can I work remotely in Vietnam on the 90-day e-visa?

acceptedAnswer.text: This exists in a legal gray area. The e-visa is officially for tourism and does not authorize employment by a Vietnamese company. However, it is widely used by remote professionals whose work meets a specific profile: you work for foreign companies, serve foreign clients, and your income is paid entirely outside of Vietnam. As long as your activities do not involve taking local jobs, it is a generally accepted practice.

mainEntity.name: Does staying 90 days in Vietnam make me a tax resident?

acceptedAnswer.text: No, a single 90-day stay does not. Vietnam's primary test for tax residency is physical presence for **183 days or more** within a calendar year or a 12-month period. However, this visa is a significant contributor to that total. You are responsible for tracking your cumulative days if you plan to return to Vietnam later in the same year to avoid inadvertently crossing the threshold.

mainEntity.name: What is the official government website for the Vietnam 90-day e-visa?

acceptedAnswer.text: The only official channel is the National Portal on Immigration, accessible via two domains: `https://thithucdientu.gov.vn` and `https://evisa.gov.vn`. Avoid all third-party visa agencies, which add unnecessary fees and data privacy risks.

mainEntity.name: What documents should I keep from my trip for tax purposes?

acceptedAnswer.text: Maintain a dedicated digital folder containing five key items to create an audit-proof record: the approved e-visa PDF, inbound and outbound flight confirmations, and clear photos of your passport pages showing the Vietnam entry and exit stamps.

body.content: For the global professional, an extended stay in Vietnam is not a vacation; it's a strategic operation. The 90-day e-visa is your primary tool, but treating it with a tourist's casualness exposes you to avoidable risks, from entry denial to unforeseen tax liabilities. The difference between a seamless, productive deployment and a compliance nightmare lies in a simple mindset shift: you are the CEO of your own international operation.

This playbook provides a three-phase framework for managing your Vietnam e-visa with the precision it demands. By adopting this approach, you trade reactive worry for proactive control, ensuring your entire stay—from the initial application to your final exit stamp—is secure, compliant, and professionally managed.
Phase 1: Secure a Flawless & Compliant Entry

Your operational success begins not when you land, but with the meticulous execution of your visa application. This initial phase is about establishing a clean, verifiable record from the very first step, mitigating risk before you ever board the plane.
**Execute the Application with Precision**

Your first act of risk mitigation is to engage only with the official Vietnam Immigration Department's e-visa portal. Third-party websites, often prominent in search results, introduce unnecessary data vulnerabilities and inflated fees. Use the official government portals—`https://evisa.gov.vn` or `https://thithucdientu.gov.vn`—and treat every field with the seriousness of a legal document. Ensure your name matches your passport exactly and the photo you upload is crisp and professional. This precision is your first demonstration of diligence.
**Choose Your Entry Type Strategically**

The choice between a single-entry (25 USD) and multiple-entry (50 USD) visa is simple. For a professional, the multiple-entry option is not an expense; it's a low-cost insurance policy for operational agility. A critical client meeting in Singapore or a supply chain issue in Bangkok can arise without warning. A multiple-entry visa provides the flexibility to leave and re-enter Vietnam without restarting the application process, preserving the continuity of your operations. The trivial savings on a single-entry visa pale in comparison to the potential cost of a disrupted project.
**Establish Your Digital "Go-Bag"**
The moment your e-visa approval arrives, your documentation work begins. Immediately create a dedicated, cloud-synced folder (e.g., "Vietnam_Visa_Q3_2025") and save three key documents:

items: The official e-visa PDF.
The digital receipt for your payment.
The application confirmation email.

body.content: This disciplined record-keeping is the foundation of your compliance file. It’s not just for the immigration officer; it’s an invaluable asset when you or your accountant need to verify dates and expenses for year-end tax filings.
**Verify Your Port of Entry Before Booking**

This final check is non-negotiable. Your e-visa is valid only at a specific list of 42 designated international border gates. Booking a flight into an airport not on this approved list will result in denied entry—a catastrophic and entirely avoidable business disruption. Always cross-reference your intended airport, such as Noi Bai in Hanoi or Tan Son Nhat in Ho Chi Minh City, with the official government list before purchasing a ticket.
Phase 2: Manage Your In-Country Compliance Risks

With your flawless entry secured, the compliance landscape shifts from travel documents to your physical presence. This phase requires active management of two critical, often-overlooked risks: temporary residence registration and the activation of your global tax clock.
Mastering Temporary Residence Registration

This is the single most important administrative task upon arrival and the primary source of avoidable risk for professionals. Vietnamese law requires every foreigner to register their address with the local police for every night spent in the country.

items: **Understand the Responsibility Chain:** The obligation to register falls on the owner or manager of your accommodation. Your task is to ensure it is completed.

rows: Proactively ask for confirmation of your registration upon check-in. This is standard procedure, but verification is key.
You **must** confirm with the host *before booking* that they are able and willing to complete this registration for you.

items: **Build Your Compliance File:** Once registered, request a digital copy or a clear photo of the registration form. This is your tangible proof of compliance. Add this document to the digital "go-bag" you created for your visa.

**Recognize the Consequences:** Failure to register is not a minor oversight. Penalties can range from fines (up to 5,000,000 VND) to severe complications, including visa cancellation or difficulty with future renewals, directly impacting your operations in Southeast Asia.

body.content: The 90-Day Stay and Your Global Tax Residency Clock
A 90-day stay is a significant period that pushes you halfway toward a critical financial threshold: unintentionally becoming a tax resident.

items: **Understand Vietnam's 183-Day Rule:** Vietnam, like most countries, defines a tax resident as someone physically present for **183 days or more** in a calendar year or within 12 consecutive months. Once you cross this line, you are generally subject to Vietnamese personal income tax on your *worldwide* income.

**Beware of "Stacking" Visas:** The 90-day clock does not reset if you leave and return on a new visa within the same tax year. Each day is cumulative. If you complete one 90-day stay and later return for another, you must meticulously track your total days to avoid accidentally crossing the 183-day threshold.

**Adopt a Strategic Mindset:** This visa does not automatically create a tax liability. It does, however, demand that you become the strategic manager of your physical presence. You must consciously track your total days in Vietnam for the remainder of the tax year. This simple discipline is the firewall between a well-managed professional stay and a costly tax misstep.

body.content: Phase 3: Execute a Documented Exit for Future Audits

For many professionals, particularly Americans qualifying for the Foreign Earned Income Exclusion (FEIE), every day spent outside their home country is a valuable asset. Your departure from Vietnam is your last chance to gather the official evidence needed to defend your international status against future scrutiny. This isn't just good record-keeping; it's about building an audit-proof case.
Build Your Audit-Proof Exit File
Before boarding your flight home, consolidate the definitive proof of your time in-country. Your dedicated digital folder for the trip should contain these five essential documents:

items: Your approved e-visa PDF.
Your inbound flight itinerary (proving arrival date).
Your outbound flight itinerary (proving departure date).
A clear photo of the passport page with your Vietnam entry stamp.
A clear photo of the passport page with your Vietnam exit stamp.

body.content: Why Passport Stamps Still Matter

In a digital world, it’s easy to dismiss the value of an ink stamp. This is a mistake. While flight itineraries can be changed or disputed, a government-issued entry and exit stamp is **irrefutable proof** of your physical presence between two specific dates. An auditor can question a flight receipt; they cannot easily question an official stamp from Vietnamese immigration. Before leaving the immigration counter, glance down and ensure the stamp is present and legible. It is the most powerful piece of evidence in your file.
Reconcile Your Records Immediately

Within 48 hours of departure, while the details are fresh, update your master travel spreadsheet or tracking application. Log the exact entry and exit dates from your passport stamps. This 15-minute task prevents a massive year-end headache and ensures the integrity of your data for tax filings. This disciplined exit process transforms your visa from a simple travel pass into a documented, defensible component of your global financial strategy.
Frequently Asked Questions

items:
**How do I register my temporary residence in Vietnam as a foreigner?**
The legal obligation falls on your accommodation provider (hotel or landlord). For hotels, it's a standard part of check-in. For private rentals (e.g., Airbnb), you must confirm with the host *before booking* that they will handle the registration. Your responsibility is to verify it has been done and request a photo of the completed form for your records.

**Can I work remotely in Vietnam on the 90-day e-visa?**
This exists in a legal gray area. The e-visa is officially for tourism and does not authorize employment by a Vietnamese company. However, it is widely used by remote professionals whose work meets a specific profile: you work for foreign companies, serve foreign clients, and your income is paid entirely outside of Vietnam. As long as your activities do not involve taking local jobs, it is a generally accepted practice.

**Does staying 90 days in Vietnam make me a tax resident?**
No, a single 90-day stay does not. Vietnam's primary test for tax residency is physical presence for **183 days or more** within a calendar year or a 12-month period. However, this visa is a significant contributor to that total. You are responsible for tracking your cumulative days if you plan to return to Vietnam later in the same year to avoid inadvertently crossing the threshold.

**What is the official government website for the Vietnam 90-day e-visa?**
The only official channel is the National Portal on Immigration, accessible via two domains: `https://thithucdientu.gov.vn` and `https://evisa.gov.vn`. Avoid all third-party visa agencies, which add unnecessary fees and data privacy risks.

**What documents should I keep from my trip for tax purposes?**
Maintain a dedicated digital folder containing five key items to create an audit-proof record: the approved e-visa PDF, inbound and outbound flight confirmations, and clear photos of your passport pages showing the Vietnam entry and exit stamps.

body.content: Conclusion: From Anxiety to Agency

This entire playbook is built on a single premise: you are the CEO of your own global operation, and the 90-day e-visa is a strategic asset you must manage with precision. The anxieties that plague many professionals—fear of a compliance misstep, the risk of a fine, the worry of a future audit—are the direct result of treating a professional endeavor with a tourist's casualness.
By adopting this three-phase framework, you trade reactive worry for proactive control:

items: **Phase 1: Secure Entry** is your initial investment in risk mitigation, building a foundation of compliance before you land.
**Phase 2: In-Country Management** is your ongoing operational oversight, protecting you from the most significant legal and financial risks.
**Phase 3: Documented Exit** is your final act of diligence, securing irrefutable proof of your operational timeline for future tax reconciliation.

body.content: This methodical approach transforms your 90-day stay from a source of potential stress into a structured, predictable, and controlled business operation. The ultimate reward is the peace of mind that comes from knowing you have managed your affairs with professional seriousness, freeing you to focus completely on your goals and leverage your time in Vietnam for maximum success. You move from a state of anxiety to one of complete agency.
